words: SAN SCHRAGER: What about Mrs. Carlson's (her mother-in-law's) reputation as a
midwife?
WILLA CUNNINGS CARLSON: Well, they couldn't always get a doctor out there, the
roads were muddy and one thing another. Well economy was a factor — doctors
cost money, and if they could get somebody to do the work, why they'd get
them. And of course you never thought of charging them anything. But they
remembered her, and they would try to do things for her. She'd get many
things done for her in the way of gifts and things like that, something that
they could give. She would get things from them for helping them out. And
they would come to get her and tell her that so-and-so was sick. Or instead
of having a nurse, they didn't get a nurse at all, they'd have her even if
they had a doctor. They'd have a doctor and then call for Nrs. Carlson.
In fact the doctor got used to having her places. I remember her daughter,
Ellen Duthie, counting up one time how many places Nomma had been. She said,
"I just counted up how many babies Nomma has helped bring into the world.
She'd practically brought in this generation!" She said, when she was talking.
I don't remember the figure, but do you know it seems to me like she said
twenty-two babies at that time. But I'd better not give that as a figure.

speaker: SAN
words: Are there any times that stand out when she was called on to be a midwife,

speaker: SAN
words: situations?

speaker: SAN
words: W C: Well, the Lawrence Johnson children, after she went to the Ridge from Noscow,

speaker: SAN
words: she was up there when they were all born. I know that, that Lawrence Johnson

speaker: SAN
words: would come down for her and ask her to come. And Paul's father got so he

speaker: SAN
words: complained about it, that she'd go off and leave him. Ny goodness, he had

speaker: SAN
words: to get a meal or had to get a breakfast sometimes. But he'd get the kids

speaker: SAN
words: out, they had to get out, they learned to get breakfast pretty quick.

speaker: SAN
words: And he'd have to get them up. But they had no idea how they inconvenienced

speaker: SAN
words: him, not to say anything at all about how they inconvenienced her he NEVER

speaker: SAN
words: mentioned that. That she was inconvenienced or anything of that sort,

speaker: SAN
words: but that because she left, why, "Poor old jrie, I had to suffer." That's the

speaker: SAN
words: way he felt about it. I heard a lot of that. But there was a family that

speaker: SAN
words: lived down in the canyon by the name of Noody. Down the canyon, and she'd have

speaker: SAN
words: to go down to the railroad track, across the railroad track, and then halfway

speaker: SAN
words: up the canyon on the other side where this bench was. And there this cabin

speaker: SAN
words: was built. And I don't know how it came about that old Pete Noody bought

speaker: SAN
words: that cabin, but he bought it, and they had several small children. She went

speaker: SAN
words: down there when they had a baby, and then a little later on they drank

speaker: SAN
words: polluted water from some stream around there. Of course you know there was

speaker: SAN
words: cattle running in the canyon and everthing else. And they got typhoid fever,

speaker: SAN
words: two of the children, and one of them died, it was a little girl. And I

speaker: SAN
words: remember going to that funeral, because I was in Sunday School, and they said

speaker: SAN
words: they were going to have a funeral for the little girl. And the superintendent

speaker: SAN
words: of the Sunday School asked that all the little girls that were anywhere near

speaker: SAN
words: that girl's age — she was younger than I, quite a little bit — that they

speaker: SAN
words: would all sit up front and sing the songs that they'd sung for Children's

speaker: SAN
words: Day. That's what we did. And they had to carry that casket out of the

speaker: SAN
words: canyon. I don't know how old she was, but I think maybe about nine or ten.

speaker: SAN
words: And then the boy was sick, Nils Noody, and they used to want her to come and

speaker: SAN
words: help take care of him. I don't know why — because the mother was sick, I

speaker: SAN
words: guess. But she'd been over there helping take care of that typhoid child, and

speaker: SAN
words: there were two children that had it. And she'd go, walk over there in the

speaker: SAN
words: morning, get up and do up her work, hurry up, walk down that canyon, up

speaker: SAN
words: that side hill on the other side. Why I was just given out if I walked across

speaker: SAN
words: the canyon, believe me, I couldn't do anything more till I got a rest. I had

speaker: SAN
words: to have a rest. That was just enough for me. It was a day's work, and I

speaker: SAN
words: used to think that I was doing too much by jumping in and doing anything,

speaker: SAN
words: because I didn't get to rest too long. But my goodness I couldn't do like she

speaker: SAN
words: did! Oh she was a strong, husky woman, and she could do that. And then she'd

speaker: SAN
words: go back, carry water up the hill from the spring, they told me. She wasn't

speaker: SAN
words: doing that when I knew her, because as I told you they put that hydraulic ram

speaker: SAN
words: into this wonderful spring. By that time they had the water pumped by the ram

speaker: SAN
words: up on the hill, so she didn't have to go and carry water then. But they said

speaker: SAN
words: she did when she was going to Noody's, that she'd come back, she'd leave

speaker: SAN
words: her buckets there and she'd carry water up the hill, and then get home and

speaker: SAN
words: (sighs) milk the cows, take care of the milk and get supper and everthing else.

speaker: SAN
words: She did it. Oh, she was a worker. And I could never hope to work with her.

speaker: SAN
words: I know I did an awful big day's work after I was there.

speaker: SAN
words: I'm going to ask you an ignorant question on my part, which is why would

speaker: SAN
words: she be so sought after? What makes her a real midwife, as compared to just

speaker: SAN
words: a woman helping at the birth of a child?

speaker: SAN
words: W C: Well I gave her the name of "midwife". I think she'd hesitate to have it

speaker: SAN
words: that way. But she wasn't afraid of anything, she was a dauntless woman.

speaker: SAN
words: I don't believe there was a thing that he was afraid of. And if a doctor once

speaker: SAN
words: told her what to do, and she went someplace, she would apply that again.

speaker: SAN
words: And still she could get panicky about something, so I don't know that she

speaker: SAN
words: was — she wasn't a trained midwife, only she'd gotten a good training by

speaker: SAN
words: being calle d out so many times. I think because in that day and age

speaker: SAN
words: it was rare to find a woman who was willing to do it! And she went because

speaker: SAN
words: they asked her, because there was a need and she tried to fill it, and she

speaker: SAN
words: filled it so adequately so many times that finally she became recognized for

speaker: SAN
words: having done it. Now that's my explanation of that, because she wasn't a

speaker: SAN
words: trained midwife. She was trained in a few things that most women weren't.

speaker: SAN
words: And then she had a naturally even, nice disposition. For others I think she

speaker: SAN
words: was kind, I felt there were times when I didn't see this kindness. I knew

speaker: SAN
words: her as a warm human being who did an awful lot of good, and certainly I knew

speaker: SAN
words: more good about her than I did anything else, but I also know her as a human

speaker: SAN
words: being who had the frailties and the faults along with the good points of

speaker: SAN
words: human kind. Now that's the way I knew her. Now her daughters deified her.

speaker: SAN
words: Do you know, around the birth, what practices people followed to make

speaker: SAN
words: Sure — do you know what I'm driving at?

speaker: SAN
words: W C: Well she knew all about hot water and boiling everything that touched the

speaker: SAN
words: patient, and the clean sheets. And those Swedish women were clean. All of

speaker: SAN
words: them were clean. They knew about cleanliness. They were cleaner than the

speaker: SAN
words: American women that I knew. I'm saying an awful thing, don't for heaven's

speaker: SAN
words: sake put that down there (chuckles) or my name will be mud. (Laughs) But

speaker: SAN
words: they were so clean, and you know, they understood the principle of cleanliness,

speaker: SAN
words: and they scrubbed and scoured, and were much more particular about their

speaker: SAN
words: scrubbing and scouring I think than the ordinary woman. And so they would

speaker: SAN
words: be clean, and Paul's mother was especially so. She had cleanliness and every

speaker: SAN
words: thing that touched the patient would be clean or be scoured. But beyond

speaker: SAN
words: that I don't know what she had.

speaker: SAN
words: You said before that she believed in old wive's tales and the old ways.

speaker: SAN
words: You didn't use the word "old wive's tales", but that's sort of what I draw

speaker: SAN
words: from it. There were the old beliefs that she had, such as the case when the young

speaker: SAN
words: girl, who was buried, was she buried alive? That sort of what we might call

speaker: SAN
words: superstitions now, Are there beliefs of hers that you can think of that are

speaker: SAN
words: those old country beliefs that were brought over here?

speaker: WC
words: No, I dcn't know about that. I noticed that her daughter, Ellen, when she was

speaker: WC
words: going to have her children, that she didn*t have her mother. And of course when

speaker: WC
words: Paul 3r. was going to be born, she thought I should stay with her in Lewiston,

speaker: WC
words: and I just didn't want to. Anid I went to the hospital, but I had a very bad time.

speaker: WC
words: Things were not right, and I always thought it was a blessing I was at a hospital,

speaker: WC
words: considering what had happened. But she didn't look at it that way. She thought

speaker: WC
words: if I'd a been at home, that those things wouldn't have come up. That was her

speaker: WC
words: attitude, see. Inever knew so much about that. However(chuckles) I didn't get

speaker: WC
words: to the hospital, bad weather and an awful bad time when Bruce was born. And I

speaker: WC
words: was in the country and wasn't prepared for this at all. But when it came along, I

speaker: WC
words: did have one awful good neighbor, and I called her, and she came down to help

speaker: WC
words: the doctor. But we had one terribly good doctor, and he told Paul what to get in

speaker: WC
words: town, so we had all the disinfectants and everything we could possibly want or

speaker: WC
words: need. And he came up and came in the door scolding like sin, to think I hadn't

speaker: WC
words: gotten out of there. But that was over in Kooskia.

speaker: SS
words: There's a mention of your mother-in-law in They Came to a Ridge. It says that

speaker: SS
words: she took flowers from her hat when there were no fresh flowers, when a baby had

speaker: SS
words: died, to put in the casket. Did you ever hear that about her?

speaker: WC
words: No. I never did hear that. I didn't know that she did. I wouldn't say she didn't

speaker: WC
words: do it, she would do it if they didn't have any. And she might have done it. But I

speaker: WC
words: didn't know anything about it. Now, I'll tell ya, I went through, I'm not saying

speaker: WC
words: I went through anything like she did, but I mean Harris Ridge when we lived over

speaker: WC
words: in Kooskia in Idaho County, was so primitive compared to Burnt Ridge, that you know

speaker: WC
words: you'd have thought you'd gotten int primitive region for sure, by the time I

speaker: WC
words: came to live on Burnt Ridge. And of course, she had lived there for some years

speaker: WC
words: before that, and it wasn't as settled up and as good when she went out there. She

speaker: WC
words: went out there when it was worse. Oh they went out there, let's see,they built

speaker: WC
words: that house in 1915, and they'd lived in the old house, I believe five years before

speaker: WC
words: they got that, and got the ram in. But all the children were born in, Moscow,

speaker: WC
words: except the little one that died. The little girl that died. She was born after

speaker: WC
words: they went to the farm. She died of measles. But it was not so primitive, my

speaker: WC
words: goodness, I thought I'd hit a primitive, I found that what I'd complained about

speaker: WC
words: when my folks had was foolish compared to what I found over there. Because I went

speaker: WC
words: in as a schoolteacher, people though I should know. I had never officiated at a

speaker: WC
words: death. There was lots of older women. Ny mother might go to someplace, she didn't

speaker: WC
words: go much. Ny mother didn't go out much, but she did some. If someone was sick and

speaker: WC
words: dying she felt she should go and help. And she did.

speaker: WC
words: When I got to Burnt Ridge I

speaker: WC
words: was the schoolteacher and I was called on. I never will forget the first time I

speaker: WC
words: was called on. A young woman, married, and she had this baby primaturely, and it

speaker: WC
words: died. Well, that motherwas a midwife and advertised herself to be, from Finland,

speaker: WC
words: and said she had had training in Finland. There was a bunch of Finlanders up

speaker: WC
words: there and they were fine people, but, no thanks. I didn't want 'em for midwives,

speaker: WC
words: not with me. But, nevertheless, Rita didn't get out of there, oh, the roads were

speaker: WC
words: terrible and they didn't get the doctor there in time. The baby was dead. And

speaker: WC
words: they asked the doctor,"No," he said,"it's pretty big baby," and all that, but

speaker: WC
words: he said it hadn't matured. It was just too young. And if we'd have had it where we

speaker: WC
words: could put it in an incubator and all those things, it might have lived. But it

speaker: WC
words: was dead. Well she was among Finlanders, and she wasn't used to it. I represented

speaker: WC
words: to them a schoolteacher that ought to know, and to her I represented somebody like

speaker: WC
words: she, I was an American girl, an American woman. I was just a young woman, mind

speaker: WC
words: you, and I'd never taken care of, yes, Ifo ta^en care of a baby where the woman

speaker: WC
words: needed to have somebody take care of the baby after it was born and all that, but

speaker: WC
words: I didn't have anything to do with the birth. So I went and I prepared that baby for

speaker: WC
words: burial. And believe it or not, it wasn't easy. But I had this one blessed neighbor,

speaker: WC
words: oh, she was so good. And she said,"They didn't ask me but,"she said,"I think you

speaker: WC
words: need company if you go over there tonight." She said,"Do you want me along?" And

speaker: WC
words: I said,"Oh yes, I certainly do." She was the one I had to come when Bruce was

speaker: WC
words: born. So she came down and we went horseback. Oh, the roads were terrible! And

speaker: WC
words: Mavis was a baby and Paul could stay home with the baby but he couldn't do any

speaker: WC
words: thing else. And he gave me all kinds of instructions and all  warnigns

speaker: WC
words: and I don't know,, what all not, over there, and they'd gone to town, two of them

speaker: WC
words: had gone horseback.

speaker: WC
words: But they'd have to bring anything from town that they needed,

speaker: WC
words: and they had a number of things to bring. So they weren't going to get a casket.

speaker: WC
words: And the carpenter was there, he would make it. And he said,"I've got the boards out

speaker: WC
words: here, all planed and nice," he said,"they're beautiful. Sanded and they're white

speaker: WC
words: they're beautiful boards," he said,"but you tell me how to make it." Well, Mrs.

speaker: WC
words: Fitting and I both had the same idea: he might make it a plain one, but one that

speaker: WC
words: was slanted, you know, sloped toward the head and smaller at the foot. And he did

speaker: WC
words: a beautiful job on that, he really did* Of course he was a carpenter who lived up

speaker: WC
words: there in that country, an old bachelor named Dietrich. I'll never forgethim. And

speaker: WC
words: he said,"Well now, that casket's good enough for anybody." Well then they sent

speaker: WC
words: to town by the stepfather of the man she married for something to line it with.

speaker: WC
words: Well he brought up some cotton but nothing else. And I said, "Well, I've got

speaker: WC
words: something." I was going to make a driss, I think, for Nevis, oh no, it was later

speaker: WC
words: on, I think I said,"I've got something over home," and it was a yard and a half

speaker: WC
words: of material."I've got some lace." The lace was intended for a dress. I went home

speaker: WC
words: and got them. Mrs. Fitting and I fitted it in and sewed it in. "Oh no," Nrs.

speaker: WC
words: Linding said,"I'm not going to put the cotton in there. We don't need cotton." she

speaker: WC
words: said. And they had sheep's wool that she'd washed and it was snowy white. They had

speaker: WC
words: sheep and she carded that wool, she carded it so fast and laid that carded wool in

speaker: WC
words: there, it was so nice and white and fluffy. And then we put the lining in. Nrs.

speaker: WC
words: Fitting and I fitted it. We did that. We did it all. We got the little thing

speaker: WC
words: ready and made the casket, lined the casket and took care of that, and then we

speaker: WC
words: wondered what we'd do for flowers. And I said,"Well", they had som tender, young

speaker: WC
words: spruce down underneath, and we had the boys cut some of the real tender spruce.

speaker: WC
words: I took a sack of that home, a.flour sack full and made little wreaths after I

speaker: WC
words: got home. I said,"I've got ribbons and I'll tie ribbons on it."

speaker: WC
words: And my Christmas

speaker: WC
words: cactus, it had been Paul's mother's, a great big Christmas cactur was in bloom and

speaker: WC
words: there were twenty-one blooms, and they went around on that, you know, three of the

speaker: WC
words: wreaths and we had three of the prettiest wreaths you ever say, ribbons and the

speaker: WC
words: piok cactus. So when you mention putting flowers on something, now that's what

speaker: WC
words: Paul's mother would have done and so no doubt she did that, and they eviidntly knew

speaker: WC
words: something about it...Now, you see, Paul's father bought the original Carlson place,

speaker: WC
words: the original 160. He bought it as a from 3ack Driscoll, one of the Driscolls,

speaker: WC
words: And at that time, they were having babies then and there were people that left the

speaker: WC
words: ridge before I ever got there. I might have been one of those and he probably went

speaker: WC
words: with one of the men to take care of things and maybe saw all that and that would

speaker: WC
words: have been just the kind of a thing she'd done, and I expect she did. I wouldn't

speaker: WC
words: be at all surprised. But I didn't happen to see it, to know about that. But we did

speaker: WC
words: this for this baby. Of course, the mother was overwhelmed because she wanted

speaker: WC
words: something done for it, and she felt so lonely and so alone. But then,that was up

speaker: WC
words: there and that was quite a primitive place, believe me. Oh, Mrs. Fitting and I went

speaker: WC
words: on a lot of errands, two or three times we went on horseback.(chuckles) When we

speaker: WC
words: got together, we'd always laugh and talk about the piaces that we'd been to, where

speaker: WC
words: we'd be called out. But now, that's not Latah county.

speaker: SS
words: What I wanted to ask you about, we touched on it a few times, but it really gets

speaker: SS
words: down to something I think is very important.What was the difference in character

speaker: SS
words: among different nationalities, the different ridges and different places? I'm

speaker: SS
words: wondering what difference the cultural backgrounds did make to, let's say, the

speaker: SS
words: Swedes or Irish as compared to each other, Norwegian people. What were some of

speaker: SS
words: the differences?

speaker: WC
words: There weren't so many differences, and the differences were soon obliterated.

speaker: WC
words: It

speaker: WC
words: didn't take long. It was like the Bostonians, you know. The ones that lived up on

speaker: WC
words: the hill. The shanty Irish as they were called when they came over, well they soon

speaker: WC
words: found the shanty Irish could live in those big fine homes back there just as well

speaker: WC
words: as the rich something else. I don't know what they were, but the haughty English

speaker: WC
words: that they'd have so muen* So, just about the same. The differences melted away.

speaker: WC
words: At first there was the language barrier, and of course they spent a lot of time

speaker: WC
words: trying to imitate the other fellow, the way he talked. Some time was given to

speaker: WC
words: that, that this Swede said that when he went in where the butcher was. And little

speaker: WC
words: things that they might think was a differences. I know there are little things that

speaker: WC
words: are differences, little thingsin what youkeep. Ao theywere all so small, find the

speaker: WC
words: way things were cooked and the way thing were served, that served as a medium of

speaker: WC
words: conversation for awhile, you know, among them. But then they began to taste things

speaker: WC
words: and they found that the Swedes were awful good cooks, and pretty soon the young

speaker: WC
words: folks were going over because they had such good feeds over there. And they ate so

speaker: WC
words: many cookies. Well, they never saw so many cookies in their lives. But they were

speaker: WC
words: such good cookies. "Get the recipe." "All right", and pretty soon the English

speaker: WC
words: were making the Swedish cookies and so were the others, Norwegians were making

speaker: WC
words: this, that, or the other.Lutefisk, they were cooking lutefisk at Christmastime.

speaker: WC
words: "Well what in the dickens is lutefisk?" Some of these people had never heard of

speaker: WC
words: it. "It's Quod." "Well, find out how they make it and we'll make some." Pretty

speaker: WC
words: soon they were having lutefisk at Christmastime. And now, potatiskerv, you know

speaker: WC
words: what it is? Well it's really pork sausage with some beef mixed in, I guess.

speaker: WC
words: Anyway they wanted it quite lean, and it's ground very fine and put through the...

speaker: WC
words: I. nave helped my mother-in-law make it. And she always wanted the sausage ground

speaker: WC
words: very fine, that and potatoes, and the seasonings that were put in. There were

speaker: WC
words: seasonings put in, of course, lots of pepper, and maybe red pepper and allspice,

speaker: WC
words: I think.She was always particular about the spices that were put in. You aaed

speaker: WC
words: allspice with meats but you didn't use allspice with fruits, and things like that

speaker: WC
words: because they used both of spice. Then they stuff them, they got a little instrum

speaker: WC
words: ent, you buy them at a hardware stare. And those are what you stuff the sausages

speaker: WC
words: with. And they are the intestines, of course, that they clean out. And it's not

speaker: WC
words: near as bad to clean'em, dh I thought, at first we all had our noses turned up,

speaker: WC
words: because we couldn't possibly eat anything that was inside that, never thinking

speaker: WC
words: about all the weiners and things of theat sort that were being consumed, you know.

speaker: WC
words: And then when they found that out, of course, it wasn't bad then. But then how

speaker: WC
words: they could clean them, and we used to clean them at home. My mother knew how to

speaker: WC
words: clean them, put them on this thing and then just roll back the intestine and it was

speaker: WC
words: clean and tear it out. Tear out the inside, well then nothing could be cleaner.

speaker: WC
words: And they were soaked in salt water for so long a time and then washed and dried,

speaker: WC
words: and then if a little time elapsed before you used them, you had to soak them again

speaker: WC
words: so they'd be wet. Then you'd stuff that with the sausage. And that's potatiskerv.

speaker: WC
words: And it's very good. That's made every Christmas in every Swedish home. And my, it

speaker: WC
words: wasn't long til ever English home was having potatiskerv too.

speaker: SS
words: How did the Irish from Driscoll Ridge and the Swedish people from the nearby

speaker: SS
words: areas hit it off from the begining?

speaker: WC
words: Fine, fine. They never had any trouble. But the Swedes and the Irish, and the

speaker: WC
words: Irish took in everything that was American otherwise that had lived there for a

speaker: WC
words: year or two, you know, if the Irish didn't have enough men to fill out. They had

speaker: WC
words: a tug-of-war to see which could outpull the other. And those Swedish lumberj acks

speaker: WC
words: they could usually pull pretty hard, but of course, there were lots of Irish

speaker: WC
words: lumberjacks too, you know, clear back to Paul Bunyan. They'd take those brawny

speaker: WC
words: Irishmen and it was quite a thing to see which one could pull the other one. But

speaker: WC
words: they always called it the Swedes agoainst the Irish. But the Irish cheated. They

speaker: WC
words: had everything in the way of big men. 0 course, they'd been here a long time and

speaker: WC
words: could talk the language.

speaker: SS
words: Was this just on celebration days?

speaker: WC
words: Celebration days, yes, taht was all. Oh sometimes we'd hear about fighting, they'd

speaker: WC
words: get into, more or less good-natured when they got started. Sometimes they'd get

speaker: WC
words: reptty rough, but there was always somebody around to bring them into order. I

speaker: WC
words: never heard of anybody getting seriously hurt or anything on the fun days.

speaker: SS
words: I know in a lot of pioneering areas, those people who were English-speaking often

speaker: SS
words: tended to regard themselves as superior in the begining, /because the foreigners

speaker: SS
words: can't speak English well.

speaker: WC
words: Oh yes, in the begining, like Wollmer, you know, the ones that couldn't say

speaker: WC
words: Vollmer, were looked down upon as though there was something the matter with them,

speaker: WC
words: in a way you might say. But believe me, wait til the next generation comes up,

speaker: WC
words: you don't. Oh yes, even as late as after I'd gone back teaching,no it was after

speaker: WC
words: I'd gone back teaching, made it worse. There were a couple of children who had been

speaker: WC
words: taught Swedish in a Swedish home, because they thought when they went to school,

speaker: WC
words: they would lose it, because they always did. Now Paul had never said a word of

speaker: WC
